coredata.dc:title: Synthesis and electrochemical properties of CuS/C-dots microflower for high-performance supercapacitor
coredata.dc:creator: Sarasamreen, I.
Shajahan, Shanavas
Kumar, S. Arun
Haija, Mohammad Abu
Ramesh, R.
Anbarasan, P.M.
coredata.dc:description: Transition metal sulfides have recently captured the utmost attention as an energy storage device owing to their excellent electrochemical activity and conductivity. The amalgamation of carbonaceous materials along with transition metal sulfides was found to do wonders in the field of supercapacitors by amplifying the surface area, conductivity, capacitance, and stability of the active electrode material. In the present work microflower like CuS and CuS/C-dots composite was synthesized via the facile hydrothermal method. Numerous characterization techniques like XRD, SEM, TEM, XPS, and EDS were used to study the properties of the as-prepared samples. The CuS/C-dots composite as an active anode material exhibited an outstanding electrochemical performance with a superior specific capacitance of 2642 Fg−1 at a current density of 2 Ag−1. Furthermore, CuS/C-dots composite material displayed excellent capacitance retention of 58 % after 2000 cycles at a current density of 10 Ag−1. The superior charge transfer kinetics, unique structure, and conductivity of CuS/C-dots composite make it a remarkable material for electrochemical energy storage devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Transition metal sulfides
CuS/C-dots composite
Microflower
Hydrothermal method
Supercapacitor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic interpretation for the synthesis of CuS/C-dots microflowers.
XRD patterns of (a) CuS and (b) CuS/C-dot composite.
FESEM images of (a, b) pure CuS; (c, d) CuS/C-dots; (e, f) TEM images of CuS, CuS/C-dots.
(a) FESEM image; (b–d) elemental mapping image of C, S, Cu, and (e) EDS spectra of the CuS/C-dots composite.
N2 absorption-desorption isotherms of CuS and CuS/C-dots composite.
XPS spectra of CuS/C-dots (a) survey spectra (b) Cu2p (c) S2p (d) C1s (e) O1s.
Cyclic voltammetric curves of (a) CuS and (b) CuS/C-dots for various scan rates. The charge-discharge curves of (c) CuS and (d) CuS/C-dots for different current densities.

(a) Comparison of CV curves at 10 mV/s for both CuS & CuS/C-dots. (b) GCD curves of CuS, CuS/C-dots at constant density 2 Ag−1. (c) Comparison of specific capacitance for various current densities of CuS, CuS/C-dots. (d) Cyclic performance of CuS/C-dots at a constant density of 10 Ag−1. (e) Capacitance retention of CuS, CuS/C-dots for 2000 cycles. (f) Nyquist plots of CuS & CuS/C-dots composite.
Structural parameters of CuS and CuS/C-dots composite.
Comparative electrochemical performance of various electrode materials.

head.ce:title: Synthesis and electrochemical properties of CuS/C-dots microflower for high-performance supercapacitor

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Periyar University, Salem 636 011, Tamil Nadu, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Periyar University, Salem 636 011, Tamil Nadu

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Khalifa University, P.O. Box 127788, Abu Dhabi, United Arab Emirates

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center for Catalysis and Separations, Khalifa University of Science and Technology, P.O. Box 127788, Abu Dhabi, United Arab Emirates

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Khalifa University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Center for Catalysis and Separations, Khalifa University of Science and Technology, P.O. Box 127788, Abu Dhabi, United Arab Emirates

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Transition metal sulfides have recently captured the utmost attention as an energy storage device owing to their excellent electrochemical activity and conductivity. The amalgamation of carbonaceous materials along with transition metal sulfides was found to do wonders in the field of supercapacitors by amplifying the surface area, conductivity, capacitance, and stability of the active electrode material. In the present work microflower like CuS and CuS/C-dots composite was synthesized via the facile hydrothermal method. Numerous characterization techniques like XRD, SEM, TEM, XPS, and EDS were used to study the properties of the as-prepared samples. The CuS/C-dots composite as an active anode material exhibited an outstanding electrochemical performance with a superior specific capacitance of 2642 Fg−1 at a current density of 2 Ag−1. Furthermore, CuS/C-dots composite material displayed excellent capacitance retention of 58 % after 2000 cycles at a current density of 10 Ag−1. The superior charge transfer kinetics, unique structure, and conductivity of CuS/C-dots composite make it a remarkable material for electrochemical energy storage devices.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Clean and sustainable energy conversion and storage would be the imminent technology to tackle the environmental pollution result of increased population and exhaustion of fossil fuels [1,2]. There are several energy storage systems such as Lithium-ion batteries, fuel cells, and supercapacitors. Supercapacitors have captivated researchers owing to their tremendous electrochemical performance, high specific capacitance, long stability, amazing charge/discharge rates, outstanding power density, low cost, easy operation & eco-friendly nature [3–5]. These supercapacitors are grouped based on their storage mechanism namely electrochemical double-layer capacitors (EDLC) and pseudocapacitors. In the case of EDLCs, the charges are accumulated between the electrode/electrolyte interface (i.e., electrostatically). It uses porous carbon-based materials as electrodes. Whereas pseudocapacitors store the charges electrochemically via reversible redox reaction using pseudocapacitive materials such as metal oxides, metal sulfides, and conducting polymers [6]. The specific capacitance and energy density of pseudocapacitors is superior to electrochemical double-layer capacitors. Poor cyclic stability is the main drawback of pseudocapacitors. This flaw can be rectified by constructing an electrode material that combines the perks of both EDLC and pseudocapacitors [7–9].

Nowadays transition metal sulfides have captured the attention of researchers over metal oxides due to their excellent electrical conductivity, low electronegativity, high specific capacitance, rapid redox reaction, high thermal stability, narrow bandgap, and low cost [10–12]. Numerous transition metal sulfides are there in which CuS has impressed researchers with its large theoretical capacity, remarkable electrochemical activity, affordable price, rich valance states, as well as sustainability and abundance in nature [13,14]. Till now many CuS nanostructures like nanospheres [15], nanosheets [16], nanowires [17], microflowers [18], and microspheres [19] have been investigated as electrodes for supercapacitors. The morphologies of CuS electrode material play a vital role in electrochemical performance which can increase ionic transportation. However low electrical conductivity, poor specific capacitance, lower energy density, and short life cycles are the major drawbacks of CuS nanostructures [20,21]. These shortcomings can be rectified by integrating CuS with carbon-based materials namely graphene, carbon nanotubes, and carbon quantum dots. Carbon quantum dots are quasi-spherical dimensionless nanoparticle which is used as a composite because of their enhanced electrical conductivity, high surface area, high crystallinity, excellent specific capacitance, high power densities, large edge-site effects, amazing physicochemical stability, cost-effective, easy synthesis, non-toxicity, and ecologically sound [22,23]. Previously several transition metal oxides and sulfides with Carbon quantum dots were synthesized as electrode material for supercapacitor applications. Zhu et al. synthesized CQD decorated RuO2 and exhibited a high specific capacitance of 460 Fg−1 at a density of 50 Ag−1 with excellent capacitance retention of 96.9 % after 5000 cycles at 5 Ag−1 [24]. Xu et al. reported the fabrication of CQD/NiO nanorods which delivered a capacitance of 1858 Fg−1 at 1 Ag−1 with exceptional capacity retention of 93 % over 1000 cycles at 2 Ag−1 [25]. Prasath et al. fabricated CQD-anchored Bi2O3 as a high-performance electrode with a specific capacity of 343 Cg−1 at a current density of 0.5 Ag−1 [26]. Sahoo et al. stated that the flower-like formation of CQD/NiS revealed a superior capacitance of 880 Fg−1 at a current density of 2 Ag−1 with outstanding stability for 2000 cycles [27]. Arsalani et al. investigated the synthesis of CQD/CoS2 with a high specific capacitance of 808 Fg−1 at a density of 1 Ag−1 with a very good retention of 83.9 % at 20 Ag−1 [28]. De et al. reported the successful preparation of CuS@CQD@CHNS via a single-step hydrothermal method which exhibited a superior capacitance of 618 Fg−1 at a current density of 1 Ag−1 with exceptional stability after 4000 cycles [29]. Quan et al. studied the electrochemical performance of the CuS@CQDs which displayed an exceptional specific capacitance of 920 Fg−1 at a density of 0.5 Ag−1 revealing an outstanding cyclic stability after 10,000 cycles [30].

In this work, the CuS/C-dots microflower are synthesized and their electrochemical performance was investigated. Initially, flower-like CuS nanostructure was prepared by a simple hydrothermal method. CuS/C-dots composites were synthesized via a one-pot hydrothermal method and their electrochemical properties were compared with that of the pure CuS. The composite material exhibited excellent specific capacitance than the pristine CuS. Therefore, incorporating C-dots with the pristine CuS was found to improve the activity of the electrochemical supercapacitor.

All the chemical reagents used are of analytical grade. Copper chloride (CuCl2), thiourea (CH4N2S), polyethylene glycol 4000 (PEG-4000–98 %), coconut oil, glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆-99 %), potassium hydroxide (KOH), polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF), acetylene black, ethanol (C2H5OH) were purchased from Merck, India. Nickel foam was purchased from Shanghai Zhongwei New Material, China.

At first 100 mL of coconut oil followed by 2 g of urea and 10 g of glucose were added to the beaker and stirred for 15 min at 200 °C until it turned brown, indicating the formation of a C-dot. Afterward, 30 mL of chloroform was added to the above mixture and stirred for 10 min. Then, the oil was separated from the mixture using a funnel. Finally, the as-prepared CQD was stored at 3 °C [31].

CuS/C-dots microflowers were synthesized by one-pot hydrothermal method as shown in Fig. 1 . First, 0.2 M of CuCl2 and 1.6 g of PEG 4000 were added into 40 mL deionized water and stirred for 10 min. Then, 0.1 M of thiourea was added into another beaker containing 20 mL of deionized water and stirred for 5 min. Now, the dissolved thiourea was slowly added into the above solution and stirred for 15 min. And then 2 mL of as-prepared carbon quantum dot solution was added to the above solution in a dropwise manner and stirred for 10 min. Finally, the solution mixture was poured into a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and hydrothermally treated at 150 °C for 15 h. The resultant precipitate was washed with ethanol and distilled water and dried at 60 °C for 24 h in an oven. To synthesize pristine CuS, the same procedure was followed in the absence of a carbon quantum dot solution.

Rigaku D max ultima III X-ray diffractometer (XRD) with Cu Kα source (λ = 0.15406 nm) was used to determine the crystal structure and phase formation of the prepared samples. The Morphology of the sample was investigated using FEI Nova Nano SEM 450 field-emission scanning electron microscope and FEI Tecnai G2 F20 X-TWIN transmission electron microscope. The valence states and composition of elements present in the sample were examined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy using PHI VersaProbe 4. Energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) was used for analyzing the elements using FEI Nova Nano SEM 450 instrument. The area, pore size, and volume of the samples were studied with the help of nitrogen adsorption and desorption isotherm (BET) using the Quantachrome Nova instrument.

To analyze the electrochemical performance of the prepared electrode material (active material), a standard 3-electrode cell was used in a 2 M KOH solution. The active material, acetylene black, polyvinylidene fluoride was taken in the ratio of 8:1:1 and mixed with N-methyl 2-pyrrolidinone solution to form a uniform slurry. The as-prepared slurry was coated on a nickel foam (1 cm2) and dried in an oven at 60 °C overnight which acts as a working electrode with a mass of 2 mg, while Pt wire and Ag/AgCl were used as the counter and reference electrode, respectively. The properties of the active material were investigated using the following techniques: cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanostatic charge-discharge curve (GCD), and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). The potential range of the CV curves for various scan rates (5 to 50 mV/s) was 0 to 0.6 V. The Potential range of GCD curves for different current densities (2 to 10 Ag−1) was 0 to 0.45 V. The frequency range of 0.01 Hz-100 kHz is applied for measuring the EIS. All these studies were performed in a single electrochemical workstation (Biologic SP-150, France).

The specific capacitance of the working electrode can be derived from the galvanostatic charge-discharge curves by using the equation,
(1)
C s
=
I Δ t
m Δ V
where Cs is the specific capacitance in (Fg−1), I is the current applied (A), Δ
t is the discharging time in (s), m is the mass of the active material loaded on the nickel foam in (g) and V is the potential range in (V).

The crystalline structure of the pristine CuS and CuS/C-dots composites was examined using an X-ray diffractometer. From Fig. 2a the obtained peaks of diffraction at 2θ are 27.7°, 29.3°,31.8°, 32.9°, 38.9°, 46.9°, 47.9°, 52.7°and 59.3° ascribed to (101), (102), (103), (006), (105), (107), (110), (108) and (116) crystallographic planes indicating the hexagonal structure of pure copper sulfide (JCPDS 06–0464) respectively. No significant peaks/impurities are observed in the case of CuS/C-dots composites implying that the amount of C-dot in the composite material is very less, and well dispersed with non-crystalline structure as shown in Fig. 2b. Therefore, the composite does not affect the phase purity of the as-prepared samples. The decrease in the intensity of the CuS/C-dots inferred that the C-dots are well wrapped to CuS.

The morphology of the as-prepared CuS, CuS/C-dots microflowers was examined using FESEM as shown in Fig. 3 . The FESEM image of pristine CuS exhibited microflower-like morphology with a diameter ranging from 3.0 to 6.0 μm Fig. 3a. The flower-like morphology of the CuS is formed due to, the stacking up of numerous nanosheets at the surface of the microspheres with a thickness of about 60 nm Fig. 3b. From the Fig. 3c, d we can confirm that the addition of C-dots does not modify the flower-like morphology of the composite but there is a notable rise in the number of nanosheets with a thickness of about 40 nm, revealing the high porous nature of CuS/C-dots. Therefore, its large surface area, and greater electroactive sites, are responsible for the excellent specific capacitance, high conductivity, fast electrolyte ion intercalation, and improved electrochemical energy storage properties [32,33]. Further, the crystal structure of the samples was analyzed using TEM, which showed that the successful formation of microflower-like CuS with a diameter ranging from 3.0 to 6.0 μm was obtained which is in harmony with the FE-SEM results Fig. 3e. Whereas from the TEM results of CuS/C-dots microflowers, we can see that the black dots were decorated on the surface of the micro flower, this confirms the presence of C-dots in the composites Fig. 3f. The compositions at which the elements present in CuS/C-dots microflowers are studied using EDS spectrum and mapping. From Fig. 4a-e can confirm that the presence of equally distributed Cu, S, and C elements with no other impurities in the composite inferred the successful formation of CuS/C-dots.

Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms and BJH pore size distributions were used to examine the specific surface area, pore volume, and pore size of the pristine CuS and CuS/C-dots composite. In accordance with IUPAC classification both pristine as well as composite material exhibited type IV isotherm curves with an H3 type hysteresis loop suggesting the mesoporous nature of the materials as shown in the Fig. 5 . The BET surface area calculated for both CuS and CuS/C-dots were 1.156 m2/g and 14.900 m2/g. Furthermore, the pore volume and pore size of CuS/C-dots was found to be 0.031 cm3/g and 1.701 nm while pristine CuS exhibited 0.003 cm3/g and 1.990 nm respectively. Thus CuS/C-dots composite electrode material with greater surface area and porous nature provides large number of electroactive sites on the surface. Hence facilitating faster migration of ions between electrode and electrolyte making it a superior electrode material than pristine CuS for supercapacitor applications. Physicochemical parameters like surface area, pore volume, and pore size of both CuS and CuS/C-dots are illustrated in Table 1 .

The valance states of the CuS/C-dots composite were investigated using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy Fig. 6 . The survey spectrum of the CuS/C-dots composite is represented in Fig. 6a which depicts the presence of Cu, S, C, and O elements in the micro flowers. The binding energies of the Cu2p spectra at 932.4 eV and 952.4 eV agree with the Cu 2p3/2 and Cu 2p1/2 Fig. 6b. While the peaks at 933.2 eV and 953.4 eV are attributed to Cu2+. Moreover, one satellite peak with Cu2+ Characteristics was found at 944.8 eV [34]. As shown in Fig. 6c the high-resolution spectra of S2p are deconvolved as S2− at 160.9 eV and the peaks at 161.9 eV, and 162.9 eV are depicted as S2p3/2 and S2p1/2. The peak located at 164.9 eV corresponds to S2 2−2p1/2. While the peak at 168.6 is attributed to SO4 2− indicating the oxidation state of sulfur. Thus, various chemical states of sulfur were confirmed from XPS studies [35,36]. Fig. 6d shows the XPS spectra of C1s where three prominent peaks are observed at 284.5 eV, 285.8 eV, and 288.5 eV demonstrating the presence of CC of Sp2 graphite structure carbon atom, CO (hydroxyl), and O-C=O (carboxy carbon) complying that the carbon atoms are functionalized with oxygen in C-dots [37]. Furthermore, the binding energies of O1s spectra are observed at 531.2 eV, 532.3 eV, and 533.3 eV associated with C-OH, C-O-C, and NO stating that the oxygen functional groups are absorbed on the surface of the C-dots as depicted in Fig. 6e. [28,38]. Therefore, the above XPS data validates the presence of Cu, S, C, and O denoting the successful fabrication of the CuS/C-dot microflowers.

The electrochemical performances of the pristine CuS and CuS/C-dots composites were examined using a 3-electrode system in 2 M KOH as an aqueous electrolyte. The Properties of the anode electrode material were investigated using cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD), and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). The CV curves were analyzed for different scan rates ranging from 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, and 50 mV/s with a potential range of 0 to 0.6 V for both the pristine CuS and CuS/C-dots microflowers shown in Fig. 7a, b. From the curves it is observed that a quasi-reversible faradaic reaction took place with a pair of redox peaks, indicating the pseudocapacitive nature of the electrode material [39]. Identical redox peaks obtained from the CV curves ascribed the high reversible nature of the CuS/C-dots electrode material. As the scan rate increases, there is a change in reduction and oxidation peak potential implying that the polarization of the electrode decreases which promotes fast electron transport and charge separation [16]. The integrated area under CV curves for CuS/C-dots composites was found to be larger than the pristine CuS suggesting that interlinking between the adjacent microflowers increased. The CV curves showed superior current density for the CuS/C-dots electrode than the CuS electrode because of the large electroactive surface which is accountable for its high capacitive nature. Introducing C-dots in the composite material was found to escalate the electrical conductivity as well as reduce the internal resistance because of its porous nature. They tend to expedite the charge transfer kinetics of the electrons and ions. Thus, improving the electrochemical performance of the composite electrode material. The redox reaction responsible for the charge-discharge mechanism between the working electrode and electrolyte is displayed in the equation as
(2)
CuS +
OH −
↔ CuSOH +
e
− 1

Galvanostatic charge/discharge curves for both pristine CuS and CuS/C-dots microflowers were conducted to find the specific capacitance of the electrode material for various current densities from 2Ag−1 to 10Ag−1 with the potential range 0 to 0.45 V, respectively. The GCD curves depicted a non-linear plateau shape for both CuS and CuS/C-dots suggesting pseudocapacitive behavior and reversible redox reactions occurred at the surface of the electrode during charge/discharge cycles. From GCD curves Fig. 7c, d it is observed that an increase in current density decreases the specific capacitance of the electrode material implying that at higher current densities the availability of active ions for electrode/electrolyte interaction is not sufficient. The capacitance of both composite and pristine CuS electrode material was calibrated using the Eq. (1). The calculated specific capacitances of CuS/C-dots microflower at the current densities 2 Ag−1, 4 Ag−1, 6 Ag−1, 8 Ag−1, 10 Ag−1 were 2642 Fg−1, 2034 Fg−1, 1453 Fg−1, 1039 Fg−1, 711 Fg−1 and for pure CuS microflower were 1145 Fg−1, 900 Fg−1, 751 Fg−1, 647 Fg−1, 564 Fg−1. Therefore, CuS/C-dots composite electrode exhibited an amazing capacitance than the pure CuS electrode. The superior specific capacitance of the CuS/C-dots is due to the fast diffusion and migration of electrolytic ions at low current density (i.e.) more charges are stored at the surface of the active materials because of the inclusion of C-dots than the pristine CuS. These results are in harmony with that of the CV curves. The resultant capacitance of this work is compared with that of the previously reported material with C-dots as a composite is shown in Table 2 .

Fig. 8a, b represents the comparison cyclic voltammetry curves at a scan rate of 10 mV/s and charge-discharge curves at a current density of 2 Ag−1 for pure CuS and CuS/C-dots microflowers. The calculated specific capacitances for CuS and CuS/C-dots illustrated that the composite CuS/C-dots electrode exhibited a remarkable specific capacitance of 2642 Fg−1 while the capacitance of the pure CuS electrode material is 1145 Fg−1 respectively. The analogy of capacitance acquired for both CuS/C-dots composites and pure CuS for numerous current densities ranging from 2 Ag−1,4 Ag−1,6 Ag−1,8 Ag−1,10 Ag−1 were emphasized in Fig. 8c. For application purposes, the stability of the electrode material plays a significant role. The cyclic stability of the as-prepared CuS/C-dots electrode was investigated as shown in Fig. 8d at 10 Ag−1 for 2000 GCD cycles. Moreover, the above GCD cycle exhibits an excellent coulombic efficiency of almost 99.44 %. The electrode material of CuS with 2 mL of the C-dots composite exhibited 58 % capacitance retention, CuS with 1 mL of the C-dots composite showed 44 % retention while the bare CuS had 35 % retention at 10 Ag−1 after 2000 cycles as represented in Fig. 8e, respectively. Thus, the outstanding electrochemical performance of the CuS/C-dots microflower reveals that the increasing quantity of C-dots was found to have innumerable electroactive sites which fastens the diffusion of ions as well as improved the surface area, conductivity, electrochemical activity, and stability of the electrode material.

The electrical conductivity and electrode resistance of the samples CuS and CuS/C-dots micro flowers were analyzed in the frequency range 0.01 Hz-100 kHz using Electrochemical Impendence spectroscopy (EIS). The Nyquist plots of the electrode materials were shown in Fig. 8f which is composed of the semicircle at a higher frequency region in the electrode/electrolyte interface described as charge transfer resistance (Rct). The oblique line at the low-frequency region depicts the Warburg impedance (ZW) related to the diffusion resistance of ions. While the electronic and ionic resistance of the electrode/electrolyte (Rs) is calculated from the intercept of the real axis (Z′) with the N-q plot [45–47]. The calculated Rct values were 2.08 Ω and 0.47 Ω for pristine CuS and CuS/C-dots microflower, respectively. The CuS/C-dots electrode material displayed low charge transfer resistance than the pure CuS electrode. Thus, the high electronic conductivity of C-dots accelerates the rapid charge transfer kinetics, as well as faster ion diffusion because of the larger slope of the vertical line at the low-frequency region.

The consecutive points are accountable for the exceptional electrochemical performance of the CuS/C-dots electrode material. (i) The mesoporous nature and improved surface area of the CuS/C-dots electrode material supplying more active sites for diffusion/transport of ions during reversible redox reactions rise the specific capacitance of the material. (ii) Low conductivity of the CuS electrode material was enhanced by the inclusion of the conductive carbon-based C-dots which boost ups the transport of ion/electrons between the electrode and electrolyte and decreases the transfer resistance. Hence improving the rate capability. (iii) No change in volume during the charge/discharge cycles of CuS/C-dots electrode tends to elevate the cyclic stability of the material. (iv) C-dots with oxygen-containing functional groups mounted on the surface of CuS support the wettability of the electrode material.

Briefly, the micro flower-like CuS/C-dots composite was fabricated by using the one-step hydrothermal method as an active electrode material for supercapacitor application. The CuS/C-dots composite material showed superior electrochemical performance than the pristine CuS. The flower-like CuS/C-dots composite as an anode material displayed a remarkable specific capacitance of 2642 Fg−1 at 2 Ag−1 when compared to that of the pristine CuS. Besides the composite anode electrode material exhibited 58 % capacitance retention for 2000 charge/discharge cycles at 10 Ag−1. Therefore, the addition of C-dots with CuS has enhanced electrical conductivity, specific capacitance, charge transport, rate capability, and stability. Hence, these results illustrate the outstanding electrochemical properties of CuS/C-dots composite as a potential and amazing electrode for high-performance supercapacitors.

I. Sarasamreen: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Shanavas Shajahan: Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing, Resources. S. Arun Kumar: Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Mohammad Abu Haija: Formal analysis, Visualization. R. Ramesh: Resources, Formal analysis, Data curation, Writing – review & editing. P.M. Anbarasan: Conceptualization, Supervision, Validation, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no competing financial interests.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Current progress achieved in novel materials for supercapacitor electrodes: mini review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Najib and E. Erdem, “Current progress achieved in novel materials for supercapacitor electrodes: Mini review,” Nanoscale Adv., vol. 1, no. 8, pp. 2817–2827, 2019, doi: 10.1039/c9na00345b.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Review of energy storage systems for electric vehicle applications: issues and challenges

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. A. Hannan, M. M. Hoque, A. Mohamed, and A. Ayob, “Review of energy storage systems for electric vehicle applications: Issues and challenges,” Renew. Sustain. Energy Rev., vol. 69, no. August 2016, pp. 771–789, 2017, doi: 10.1016/j.rser.2016.11.171.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Advances and challenges for flexible energy storage and conversion devices and systems

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Li, Z. Wu, S. Yuan, and X. B. Zhang, “Advances and challenges for flexible energy storage and conversion devices and systems,” Energy Environ. Sci., vol. 7, no. 7, pp. 2101–2122, 2014, doi: 10.1039/c4ee00318g.
T. Brousse et al., “Materials for electrochemical capacitors,” Springer Handbooks, pp. 495–561, 2017, doi: 10.1007/978-3-662-46657-5_16.
C. Liu, F. Li, M. Lai-Peng, and H. M. Cheng, “Advanced materials for energy storage,” Adv. Mater., vol. 22, no. 8, pp. 28–62, 2010, doi: 10.1002/adma.200903328.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Supercapacitor energy storage device using biowastes: a sustainable approach to green energy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Mensah-Darkwa, C. Zequine, P. K. Kahol, and R. K. Gupta, “Supercapacitor energy storage device using biowastes: A sustainable approach to green energy,” Sustain., vol. 11, no. 2, 2019, doi: 10.3390/su11020414.

S. Karthikeyan, B. Narenthiran, A. Sivanantham, L. D. Bhatlu, and T. Maridurai, “Supercapacitor: Evolution and review,” Mater. Today Proc., vol. 46, no. xxxx, pp. 3984–3988, 2020, doi: 10.1016/j.matpr.2021.02.526.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrochemical supercapacitors for energy storage and conversion

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. K. Kim, S. Sy, A. Yu, and J. Zhang, “Electrochemical Supercapacitors for Energy Storage and Conversion,” Handb. Clean Energy Syst., pp. 1–25, 2015, doi: 10.1002/9781118991978.hces112.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Materials for energy storage: review of electrode materials and methods of increasing capacitance for supercapacitors

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: E. E. Miller, Y. Hua, and F. H. Tezel, “Materials for energy storage: Review of electrode materials and methods of increasing capacitance for supercapacitors,” J. Energy Storage, vol. 20, no. February, pp. 30–40, 2018, doi: 10.1016/j.est.2018.08.009.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent progress and emerging challenges of transition metal sulfides based composite electrodes for electrochemical supercapacitive energy storage

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Theerthagiri et al., “Recent progress and emerging challenges of transition metal sulfides based composite electrodes for electrochemical supercapacitive energy storage,” Ceram. Int., vol. 46, no. 10, pp. 14317–14345, 2020, doi: 10.1016/j.ceramint.2020.02.270.
